29 This is what the king says: Do not let Hezekiah fool you. He cannot save you from my hand.
30 Do not let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD when he says, 'The LORD will surely save us. This city will not fall to the king of Assyria.'

31 Do not listen to Hezekiah, for this is what the king of Assyria says: Make peace with me and come out to me. Then every one of you will eat from his own vine and his own fig tree. You will drink water from his own water tank.

32 Then I will come and take you to a land like your own — a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards, a land of olive trees and honey. You will live and not die. Do not listen to Hezekiah. He fools you when he says, 'The LORD will save us.'
33 Has the god of any nation ever saved his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?
34 Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah? Did they save Samaria from my hand?
35 Which of all the gods of these lands has saved his land from my hand? How then can the LORD save Jerusalem from my hand?"
36 But the people stayed silent and did not answer a word. Hezekiah had commanded, "Do not answer him."
37 Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah the palace manager, Shebna the writer, and Joah son of Asaph the record keeper came to Hezekiah. Their clothes were torn. They told him the words of the Rabshakeh.

title: 2 Kings 19
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 When King Hezekiah heard this, he tore his clothes. He put on rough cloth and went into the house of the LORD.
2 He sent Eliakim the palace manager, Shebna the writer, and the leading priests. They all wore rough cloth. They went to the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz.
3 They told him, "Hezekiah says this: Today is a day of trouble, shame, and pain. Children are ready to be born, but there is no strength to bring them out.

4 Maybe the LORD your God heard all the words of the Rabshakeh. His master, the king of Assyria, sent him to insult the living God. Maybe the LORD your God will punish him for those words. So please pray for the people who are still left."
5 The servants of King Hezekiah went to Isaiah.
6 Isaiah replied, "Tell your master that the LORD says this: 'Do not be afraid of the words you heard. The servants of the king of Assyria have insulted Me with them.
7 Look, I will put a feeling in him. He will hear a rumor and go back to his own land. There I will make him fall by the sword.'"
8 The Rabshakeh heard that the king of Assyria had left Lachish. So he went back and found the king fighting against Libnah.
9 Then Sennacherib heard a warning about Tirhakah king of Cush. People said, "Look, he is coming to fight against you." So Sennacherib sent messengers to Hezekiah again. They said,
10 "Say this to Hezekiah king of Judah: 'Do not let your God, whom you trust, trick you. Do not believe Him when He says Jerusalem will not fall to the king of Assyria.
11 You have heard what the kings of Assyria did to all the other lands. They destroyed them completely. Will you escape?
12 My fathers destroyed many nations. Did the gods of those nations save them? Think of Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, and the people of Eden in Telassar.
13 Where are the kings of Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah?'"
14 Hezekiah took the letter from the messengers and read it. Then he went up to the house of the LORD and spread it open before the LORD.
15 Hezekiah prayed before the LORD: "O LORD, God of Israel, You sit on the throne above the cherubim. You alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth. You made the heavens and the earth.
16 Turn Your ear to us, O LORD, and hear. Open Your eyes, O LORD, and see. Listen to the words Sennacherib sent to insult the living God.
17 It is true, O LORD. The kings of Assyria have destroyed these nations and their lands.
18 They have thrown the gods of those nations into the fire. They destroyed them, because they were not gods. They were only wood and stone, made by human hands.
19 Now, O LORD our God, please save us from his hand. Then all the kingdoms of the earth will know that You alone, O LORD, are God."
20 Then Isaiah son of Amoz sent a message to Hezekiah. "The LORD, the God of Israel, says this: I have heard your prayer about Sennacherib king of Assyria.
21 This is the word the LORD has spoken against him: 'The Virgin Daughter of Zion looks down on you and laughs at you. The Daughter of Jerusalem shakes her head behind you.
22 Whom have you insulted and mocked? Against whom have you raised your voice? Against whom have you lifted your proud eyes? Against the Holy One of Israel!

23 Through your servants you have insulted the Lord. You have said, "With my many chariots I went up the highest mountains. I reached the far peaks of Lebanon. I cut down its tallest cedars and its best cypress trees. I reached its farthest places, its thickest forests.
24 I have dug wells and drunk water in foreign lands. With my feet I have dried up all the streams of Egypt."
25 Have you not heard? I planned this long ago. In days of old I decided it. Now I have made it happen. You crush strong cities into piles of stones.
26 So their people have no power. They are dismayed and ashamed. They are like plants in the field, like soft green shoots. They are like grass on the rooftops, burned before it grows.
27 But I know when you sit down. I know when you go out and come in. I know your anger against Me.
28 Your anger and pride against Me have reached My ears. So I will put My hook in your nose. I will put My bit in your mouth. I will send you back the way you came.'

29 "And this will be a sign to you, O Hezekiah: This year you will eat what grows by itself. In the second year you will eat what grows from that. But in the third year you will plant and harvest. You will plant vineyards and eat their fruit.
30 The people of Judah who are left will again grow roots below and bear fruit above.
31 A remnant will come out of Jerusalem. Survivors will come out of Mount Zion. The strong love of the LORD of Hosts will do this.

32 So the LORD says this about the king of Assyria: 'He will not enter this city. He will not shoot an arrow into it. He will not come near it with a shield. He will not build a dirt ramp to attack it.
33 He will go back the way he came. He will not enter this city,' declares the LORD.
34 'I will defend this city and save it. I will do it for My own sake and for the sake of My servant David.'"
35 That very night the angel of the LORD went out. He struck down 185,000 men in the camp of the Assyrians. When the people got up the next morning, they saw all the dead bodies.
36 So Sennacherib king of Assyria broke camp and went away. He returned to Nineveh and stayed there.

37 One day he was worshiping in the temple of his god Nisroch. His sons Adrammelech and Sharezer killed him with the sword. Then they ran away to the land of Ararat. And his son Esar-haddon became king after him.

title: 2 Kings 20
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 In those days Hezekiah became very sick and near death. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him. He said, "This is what the LORD says: 'Get your house ready. You will die. You will not get well.'"
2 Then Hezekiah turned his face to the wall. He prayed to the LORD, saying,
3 "Please, O LORD, remember how I have lived for you with a true heart. I served you with all my heart. I did what was good in your sight." And Hezekiah cried hard.
4 Before Isaiah had left the middle yard, the word of the LORD came to him, saying,

5 "Go back. Tell Hezekiah the leader of my people: 'This is what the LORD, the God of your father David, says: I have heard your prayer. I have seen your tears. I will heal you for sure. On the third day from now, you will go up to the house of the LORD.
6 I will add fifteen years to your life. I will save you and this city from the king of Assyria. I will defend this city for my own sake and for my servant David's sake.'"
7 Then Isaiah said, "Bring a paste of figs." So they brought it and put it on the sore. And Hezekiah got well.
8 Now Hezekiah had asked Isaiah, "What will be the sign? How will I know the LORD will heal me? How will I go up to the house of the LORD on the third day?"
9 And Isaiah had answered, "This will be your sign from the LORD that he will keep his promise: Do you want the shadow to go forward ten steps, or back ten steps?"
10 "It is easy for the shadow to go forward ten steps," answered Hezekiah. "But it is not easy for it to go back ten steps."
11 So Isaiah the prophet called out to the LORD. And the LORD brought the shadow back ten steps on the stairs of Ahaz.
12 At that time Merodach-baladan son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent letters and a gift to Hezekiah. For he had heard about Hezekiah's sickness.

13 And Hezekiah welcomed the messengers. He showed them everything in his room of treasures — the silver, the gold, the spices, and the fine oil. He also showed his weapon room and all his storage rooms. Hezekiah showed them everything in his palace and in all his kingdom.
14 Then the prophet Isaiah went to King Hezekiah. He asked, "Where did those men come from? What did they say to you?" "They came from a far country," Hezekiah replied, "from Babylon."
15 "What have they seen in your palace?" Isaiah asked. "They have seen everything in my palace," answered Hezekiah. "I showed them all my treasures."
16 Then Isaiah said to Hezekiah, "Hear the word of the LORD:
17 The time will come for sure. Everything in your palace will be taken to Babylon. All that your fathers have saved will be carried away. Nothing will be left, says the LORD.
18 And some of your own sons will be taken away. They will become palace servants for the king of Babylon."
19 But Hezekiah said to Isaiah, "The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good." For he thought, "Will there not be peace and safety while I live?"
20 As for the rest of the acts of Hezekiah, with all his strong deeds — how he built the pool and the tunnel to bring water into the city — are they not written in the Book of the Kings of Judah?
21 And Hezekiah died like his fathers. His son Manasseh became king in his place.

title: 2 Kings 21
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Manasseh was twelve years old when he became king. He ruled in Jerusalem for fifty-five years. His mother's name was Hephzibah.
2 He did evil in the eyes of the LORD. He followed the hated practices of the nations the LORD had driven out before the Israelites.

3 He rebuilt the high places of worship that his father Hezekiah had destroyed. He built altars for Baal. He made an Asherah pole, just as King Ahab of Israel had done. He worshiped and served all the stars of heaven.
4 Manasseh also built altars in the house of the LORD. The LORD had said about this place, "In Jerusalem I will put My Name."
5 In both courtyards of the house of the LORD, he built altars to all the stars of heaven.

6 He burned his own son in the fire as an offering. He used magic and fortune-telling. He spoke with people who talk with the dead and spirits. He did great evil in the eyes of the LORD and made Him angry.

7 Manasseh took the carved Asherah pole he had made and set it up in the temple. The LORD had said to David and his son Solomon about this temple, "In this temple and in Jerusalem I will place My Name forever. I have chosen Jerusalem out of all the tribes of Israel.
8 I will never again make the Israelites leave the land I gave to their fathers. But they must obey all I commanded them. They must keep the whole Law that My servant Moses gave them."
9 But the people did not listen. Manasseh led them the wrong way. They did greater evil than the nations the LORD had destroyed before the Israelites.
10 The LORD spoke through His servants the prophets. He said,
11 "Manasseh king of Judah has done all these hated things. He has acted worse than the Amorites before him. He has made Judah sin with his idols.
12 So this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Look, I am bringing such trouble on Jerusalem and Judah. The news will ring in the ears of all who hear it.

13 I will stretch the measuring line of Samaria over Jerusalem. I will use the plumb line of the house of Ahab against it. I will wipe out Jerusalem as a person wipes out a bowl. I will wipe it and turn it upside down.
14 I will leave the people who are still Mine. I will give them into the hands of their enemies. They will become things taken in war by all their enemies.
15 They have done evil in My sight. They have made Me angry from the day their fathers came out of Egypt until today.'"
16 Manasseh also killed many innocent people. He filled Jerusalem from end to end with their blood. He also led Judah into sin, doing evil in the eyes of the LORD.
17 The rest of the acts of Manasseh, all he did, and his sin are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah.
18 Manasseh died and joined his fathers. He was buried in his palace garden, the garden of Uzza. His son Amon became king in his place.
19 Amon was twenty-two years old when he became king. He ruled in Jerusalem for two years. His mother's name was Meshullemeth daughter of Haruz. She was from Jotbah.
20 He did evil in the eyes of the LORD, as his father Manasseh had done.
21 He walked in all the ways of his father. He served and worshiped the idols his father had served.
22 He turned away from the LORD, the God of his fathers. He did not walk in the way of the LORD.
23 The servants of Amon made a secret plan against him. They killed the king in his palace.
24 But the people of the land killed all those who had planned against King Amon. They made his son Josiah king in his place.
25 The rest of the acts of Amon and all he did are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah.
26 He was buried in his tomb in the garden of Uzza. His son Josiah became king in his place.

title: 2 Kings 22
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Josiah was eight years old when he became king. He ruled in Jerusalem for thirty-one years. His mother's name was Jedidah daughter of Adaiah. She was from Bozkath.
2 Josiah did what was right before the LORD. He walked in all the ways of his father David. He did not turn away to either side.
3 In the eighteenth year of his rule, King Josiah sent Shaphan the scribe to the house of the LORD. Shaphan was the son of Azaliah, the son of Meshullam. The king said,
4 "Go up to Hilkiah the high priest. Have him count the money brought into the house of the LORD. The doorkeepers have collected it from the people.
5 Let them give it to the foremen who work on the house of the LORD. The foremen will give it to the workers who repair the temple—
6 to the carpenters, builders, and stone workers. They will buy wood and cut stone to repair the temple.
7 They do not need to give a report on the money. They work with honesty."
8 Then Hilkiah the high priest said to Shaphan the scribe, "I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD!" He gave it to Shaphan, who read it.
9 Shaphan the scribe went to the king and reported, "Your servants have paid out the money found in the temple. They have given it to the workers and foremen of the house of the LORD."
10 Shaphan the scribe also told the king, "Hilkiah the priest has given me a book." And Shaphan read it to the king.
11 When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes.
12 He commanded Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Achbor son of Micaiah, Shaphan the scribe, and Asaiah the king's servant:

13 "Go and ask the LORD for me, for the people, and for all Judah. Ask about the words in this book that has been found. The LORD's great anger burns against us. Our fathers did not obey the words of this book. They did not do all that is written about us."

14 So Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Achbor, Shaphan, and Asaiah went to Huldah the prophetess. She was the wife of Shallum son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas. Shallum was the man in charge of the king's clothes. Huldah lived in Jerusalem, in the Second District.
15 Huldah said to them, "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Tell the man who sent you,'"
16 "'this is what the LORD says: I will bring trouble on this place and on its people. It will match all the words of the book that the king of Judah has read.'"
17 "'They have left Me and burned incense to other gods. They made Me angry with all the works of their hands. My anger will burn against this place. No one will stop it.'"
18 "But tell the king of Judah, who sent you to ask the LORD: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says about the words you heard:"

19 "'Your heart was soft, and you made yourself humble before the LORD. You heard what I spoke against this place and its people. I said they would become ruin and a curse. You tore your clothes and wept before Me. So I have heard you,' says the LORD."
20 "'So I will take you to your fathers. You will go to your grave in peace. Your eyes will not see all the trouble I will bring on this place.'" So they brought her answer back to the king.

title: 2 Kings 23
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Then the king called together all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.

2 He went up to the house of the LORD with all the people of Judah and Jerusalem. The priests and prophets came too — all the people, small and great. He read to them all the words of the Book of the Covenant. They had found this book in the house of the LORD.

3 The king stood by the pillar and made a covenant before the LORD. He promised to follow the LORD and keep His commands, rules, and laws. He promised this with all his heart and soul. He promised to do the words of the covenant written in this book. And all the people joined the covenant.

4 Then the king gave orders to Hilkiah the high priest, the priests next in rank, and the doorkeepers. They were to take out of the temple all the objects made for Baal, Asherah, and the stars of heaven. He burned them in the fields of Kidron outside Jerusalem. He carried their ashes to Bethel.

5 Josiah also got rid of the false priests. The kings of Judah had set them up to burn incense at the high places. They burned incense in the cities of Judah and around Jerusalem. They had burned incense to Baal, the sun, the moon, the stars, and all the stars of heaven.
6 He took the Asherah pole from the house of the LORD to the Kidron Valley outside Jerusalem. He burned it there. He ground it to powder and threw the dust on the graves of the common people.
7 He also tore down the rooms of the male temple prostitutes in the house of the LORD. There the women had woven cloths for Asherah.

8 Then Josiah brought all the priests from the cities of Judah. He made the high places unclean, from Geba to Beersheba. The priests had burned incense there. He tore down the high places at the gates. These stood at the entrance of the gate of Joshua, the governor of the city. They were on the left side of the city gate.
9 The priests of the high places did not serve at the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem. But they ate unleavened bread with their fellow priests.
10 He also made Topheth unclean. Topheth was in the Valley of Ben-hinnom. Now no one could sacrifice his son or daughter in the fire to Molech.

11 He removed the horses near the entrance of the house of the LORD. The kings of Judah had given these horses to the sun. They stood in the court near the room of an official named Nathan-melech. Josiah also burned the chariots of the sun.

12 He pulled down the altars on the roof near the upper room of Ahaz. The kings of Judah had set them up. He also pulled down the altars in the two courtyards of the house of the LORD. Manasseh had set up these altars. The king ground them to dust there. He threw the dust into the Kidron Valley.

13 The king also made the high places east of Jerusalem unclean. They stood south of the Mount of Corruption. King Solomon of Israel had built them. He built one for Ashtoreth, the hated idol of the Sidonians. He built one for Chemosh, the hated idol of the Moabites. He built one for Milcom, the hated idol of the Ammonites.
14 He smashed the holy stone pillars to pieces. He cut down the Asherah poles. He covered the places with human bones.

15 He also pulled down the altar at Bethel. Jeroboam son of Nebat had set up this high place. Jeroboam had caused Israel to sin. Josiah burned the high place, ground it to powder, and burned the Asherah pole.

16 As Josiah turned, he saw tombs on the hillside. He sent men to take the bones from the tombs. He burned the bones on the altar to make it unclean. This happened just as the LORD had said. The man of God had told these things long before.

17 Then the king asked, "What is this monument I see?" The men of the city replied, "It is the tomb of the man of God from Judah. He spoke against the altar of Bethel and foretold what you have done."
18 "Let him rest," said Josiah. "No one should touch his bones." So they left his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet from Samaria.
19 Josiah did the same thing in the cities of Samaria as he had done at Bethel. He removed all the shrines of the high places. The kings of Israel had set them up and made the LORD angry.
20 On the altars he killed all the priests of the high places. He burned human bones on them. Then he returned to Jerusalem.
21 The king commanded all the people, "Keep the Passover of the LORD your God, as written in this Book of the Covenant."
22 No such Passover had been kept since the days of the judges who led Israel. Not in all the days of the kings of Israel and Judah.
23 But in the eighteenth year of King Josiah, they kept this Passover to the LORD in Jerusalem.

24 Josiah also removed the fortune tellers and spirit talkers. He removed the family gods, the idols, and all the hated things in Judah and Jerusalem. He did this to obey the words of the law in the book Hilkiah the priest had found in the house of the LORD.

25 Before Josiah, no king was like him. After him, no king was like him either. He turned to the LORD with all his heart, with all his soul, and with all his strength. He followed the whole Law of Moses.
26 Even so, the LORD did not turn away from His strong burning anger against Judah. Manasseh had made Him very angry by all he had done.

27 For the LORD had said, "I will remove Judah from My sight, just as I removed Israel. I will reject this city Jerusalem, which I chose. I will reject the temple where I said, 'My Name will be there.'"
28 The rest of the acts of Josiah and all he did are written in the Book of the Kings of Judah.

29 Near the end of Josiah's reign, Pharaoh Neco king of Egypt marched up to help the king of Assyria at the Euphrates River. King Josiah went out to face him. But Neco met him and killed him at Megiddo.

30 His servants carried his body in a chariot from Megiddo to Jerusalem. They buried him in his own tomb. Then the people of the land took Jehoahaz son of Josiah. They poured oil on him and made him king in place of his father.
31 Jehoahaz was twenty-three years old when he became king. He reigned in Jerusalem three months. His mother's name was Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah. She was from Libnah.
32 He did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as his fathers had done.
33 Pharaoh Neco put Jehoahaz in prison at Riblah in the land of Hamath. He could no longer reign in Jerusalem. Neco made Judah pay a tax of a hundred talents of silver and one talent of gold.
34 Then Pharaoh Neco made Eliakim son of Josiah king in place of his father Josiah. He changed Eliakim's name to Jehoiakim. But Neco took Jehoahaz to Egypt, and Jehoahaz died there.
35 Jehoiakim paid the silver and gold to Pharaoh Neco. To meet Pharaoh's demand, he taxed the land. He took the silver and gold from the people, each one by his wealth.
36 Jehoiakim was twenty-five years old when he became king. He reigned in Jerusalem eleven years. His mother's name was Zebidah daughter of Pedaiah. She was from Rumah.
37 He did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as his fathers had done.

title: 2 Kings 24
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 In the days of Jehoiakim's rule, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon attacked the land. So Jehoiakim became his servant king for three years. Then he turned against Nebuchadnezzar.
2 And the LORD sent groups of attackers from Chaldea, Aram, Moab, and Ammon against Jehoiakim. They came to destroy Judah. This happened just as the LORD had said through His servants the prophets.
3 All this happened to Judah because the LORD had said so. He would send them away from His sight. This was because of the sins of Manasseh and all he had done.
4 It was also because of the blood of people who did no wrong. Manasseh had filled Jerusalem with their blood. So the LORD would not forgive.
5 The rest of the deeds of Jehoiakim and all he did are written in the Book of the Kings of Judah.
6 Then Jehoiakim died like his fathers. His son Jehoiachin ruled instead of him.
7 The king of Egypt did not march out of his land again. The king of Babylon had taken all his land, from the Brook of Egypt to the Euphrates River.
8 Jehoiachin was eighteen years old when he became king. He ruled in Jerusalem three months. His mother's name was Nehushta daughter of Elnathan. She was from Jerusalem.
9 He did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as his father had done.
10 At that time the servants of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon marched up to Jerusalem. The army surrounded the city.
11 Then Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to the city while his servants were surrounding it.

12 Jehoiachin king of Judah gave himself up to the king of Babylon. His mother, his servants, his army leaders, and his officials also gave themselves up. So in the eighth year of his rule, the king of Babylon took Jehoiachin as a prisoner.

13 Nebuchadnezzar also took all the rich things from the house of the LORD and the king's palace. He cut into pieces all the gold objects that Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the LORD. This happened just as the LORD had said.

14 He took all Jerusalem far away. He took all the army leaders, all the brave fighters, and all the skilled workers and metal workers — ten thousand prisoners in all. Only the poorest people in the land stayed.
15 Nebuchadnezzar took Jehoiachin far away to Babylon. He also took the king's mother, his wives, his officials, and the important men of the land. He took them far away from Jerusalem to Babylon.
16 The king of Babylon also took to Babylon all seven thousand brave fighters, and a thousand skilled workers and metal workers. They were all strong and ready for war.
17 Then the king of Babylon made Mattaniah king instead of Jehoiachin. Mattaniah was Jehoiachin's uncle. He changed his name to Zedekiah.
18 Zedekiah was twenty-one years old when he became king. He ruled in Jerusalem eleven years. His mother's name was Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah. She was from Libnah.
19 Zedekiah did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as Jehoiakim had done.
20 All this happened in Jerusalem and Judah because of the anger of the LORD. In the end, He sent them away from His sight. And Zedekiah turned against the king of Babylon.

title: 2 Kings 25
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Kings
paragraphs: 1 In the ninth year of Zedekiah's rule, on the tenth day of the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to attack Jerusalem. He came with his whole army. They set up camp outside the city. They built a wall all around it to attack it.
2 The city was under attack until the eleventh year of King Zedekiah.
3 By the ninth day of the fourth month, hunger in the city was very bad. The people had no food.

4 Then the city wall was broken open. All the soldiers ran away by night. They went out through the gate between the two walls near the king's garden. The Babylonian army was around the city. The soldiers headed toward the Arabah.
5 But the Babylonian army chased the king. They caught him on the plains of Jericho. His whole army was scattered from him.
6 The Babylonians seized the king. They brought him to the king of Babylon at Riblah. There they passed sentence on him.
7 They killed the sons of Zedekiah while he watched. Then they put out his eyes. They tied him with bronze chains and took him to Babylon.

8 On the seventh day of the fifth month, in the nineteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar's rule over Babylon, Nebuzaradan came into Jerusalem. He was the commander of the guards and a servant of the king of Babylon.
9 He burned down the temple of the LORD, the royal palace, and all the houses of Jerusalem. He burned every important building.
10 The whole Babylonian army under the commander of the guards broke down the walls around Jerusalem.

11 Then Nebuzaradan the commander of the guards took the people who were left in the city away as captives. He also took the people who had run away to join the king of Babylon and the rest of the people.
12 But the commander of the guards left behind some of the poorest people. They were to take care of the vineyards and fields.
13 The Babylonians broke up the bronze pillars and stands and the bronze basin called "the Sea" in the temple of the LORD. They carried the bronze to Babylon.
14 They also took the pots, shovels, wick trimmers, dishes, and all the bronze tools used in the temple.
15 The commander of the guards also took the bowls for incense and water. He took everything made of pure gold or fine silver.
16 Solomon had made the two pillars, the Sea, and the movable stands for the temple of the LORD. The weight of all this bronze was too great to measure.

17 Each pillar was about 8 metres tall (eighteen cubits). The bronze top of one pillar was about 1.4 metres high (three cubits). It had a pattern of bronze pomegranates all around it. The second pillar was the same.
18 The commander of the guards also took Seraiah the head priest, Zephaniah the second priest, and the three doorkeepers.

19 From the people still in the city, he took a royal officer who was in charge of the soldiers. He took five of the king's advisors. He also took the secretary of the army commander, who had listed the people of the land. He took sixty more men who were found in the city.
20 Nebuzaradan the commander of the guards took them and brought them to the king of Babylon at Riblah.
21 There at Riblah in the land of Hamath, the king of Babylon killed them. So Judah was taken away from its own land as captives.
22 Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon put Gedaliah in charge of the people left in the land of Judah. Gedaliah was the son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan.

23 All the army commanders and their men heard that the king of Babylon had made Gedaliah their leader. So they came to Gedaliah at Mizpah. They were Ishmael son of Nethaniah, Johanan son of Kareah, Seraiah son of Tanhumeth the Netophathite, and Jaazaniah son of the Maacathite, with their men.
24 Gedaliah made a promise to them and their men. He said, "Do not be afraid of the Babylonian servants. Live in the land and serve the king of Babylon. Then it will be well with you."

25 But in the seventh month, Ishmael son of Nethaniah, the son of Elishama, came with ten men. He was a member of the royal family. They struck down and killed Gedaliah. They also killed the Judeans and Babylonians who were with him at Mizpah.
26 Then all the people, small and great, together with the army commanders, got up and ran away to Egypt. They were afraid of the Babylonians.

27 On the twenty-seventh day of the twelfth month of the thirty-seventh year of King Jehoiachin's exile, Evil-merodach became king of Babylon. In that year he let King Jehoiachin of Judah out of prison.
28 He spoke kindly to Jehoiachin. He set his throne above the thrones of the other kings who were with him in Babylon.
29 So Jehoiachin took off his prison clothes. He ate at the king's table every day for the rest of his life.
30 The king gave Jehoiachin a daily food allowance for the rest of his life.
